name: Seed
description: Elm-architecture framework, historically significant; verify current maintenance status

description.value: A Rust front-end framework for creating fast and reliable web apps with an Elm-like architecture
description.comment: README tagline; crates.io describes it as 'A Rust framework for creating web apps, using WebAssembly'.

license.comment: Licensed MIT only per crates.io metadata and the GitHub license field — a deviation from the common MIT OR Apache-2.0 dual license used by most peers.

documentation.comment: The guide site seed-rs.org is currently disabled (a third party may have acquired the domain, per README warning and seed-rs.org issue #126), so API docs on docs.rs are the primary live documentation.

latest-stable-version.comment: seed 0.10.0; checked 2026-07-19. No newer release since 2023.

latest-release.comment: crates.io publish date of seed 0.10.0; checked 2026-07-19 (over three years old).

maintenance-status.comment: README states plainly 'It's not maintained at the moment' / 'Seed is not maintained at the moment but if you want to see some features and bring a budget, feel free to contact us'. Last release 0.10.0 (2023-03-07); the only commit since is a Jan 2025 README fix (removing the dead website link). No activity in the last 6 months.

maturity.comment: README FAQ: 'As a framework, Seed is mostly feature-complete. You can build complete web apps in Seed.' API is stable and was used to build real apps; still pre-1.0 (0.10.0) and now frozen/unmaintained. Maturity assessed on API stability, not commit frequency (per RESEARCH.md).

corporate-foundation-backing.comment: No corporate/foundation sponsor; created by David O'Connor and later maintained largely by Martin Kavik under the seed-rs community org (donation/BACKERS-funded).

reactivity-model.comment: Built-in state management based on the Elm architecture (Model / Msg / update / view). Internally renders and diffs a virtual DOM, but the reactivity model exposed to users is Elm-style message passing (see uses-virtual-dom).

component-model.comment: Views/components are plain Rust functions returning Node<Msg>; nested TEA modules (own Model/Msg/update/view) are the pattern for reusable components (tea_component example). No component trait to impl.

view-authoring.comment: Views are written with per-element Rust macros (div!, p!, attrs!{}, C!, ...) forming a custom macro markup DSL — not a single html!-style macro nor an imperative builder. README highlights the macro syntax works with normal Rust linting/formatting.

fine-grained-updates.comment: Re-renders the view on each update and diffs a virtual DOM; no signal-based fine-grained node targeting.

uses-virtual-dom.comment: Seed builds a virtual DOM (El/Node tree) from the view function and diffs/patches it against the previous render, Elm/React-style.

async-await-in-components.comment: Async work is done via commands (orders.perform_cmd with async blocks) that resolve to a Msg, not by awaiting inside components/view; no suspense-style await in the component tree.

global-state-solution.comment: The single Elm Model is the app-wide state by design; there is no provide/inject context, reactive store, or dedicated global-state primitive beyond the Model.

primary-build-tool.comment: README recommends Trunk as the application bundler ('trunk serve'); projects are scaffolded with cargo-generate from the seed-quickstart template.

official-cli.comment: No first-party Seed CLI; getting-started relies on cargo-generate, trunk, and wasm-bindgen-cli.

dev-server-hmr.comment: Uses Trunk's dev server, which rebuilds and auto-refreshes the browser (live reload); no view/RSX hot reload.

minimal-wasm-bundle-size.comment: No reliably documented, methodically-measured hello-world gzipped .wasm size available; per RESEARCH.md, prefer null over an unmethodical number.

js-interop.comment: Built on wasm-bindgen with web-sys/js-sys for browser/JS APIs; README points users to web-sys for web APIs. rust_from_js / update_from_js examples show custom JS<->Rust calls.

js-interop-ergonomics.comment: Standard wasm-bindgen/web-sys path with the usual boilerplate; examples cover calling JS from Rust and vice versa, but no dedicated interop conveniences beyond the underlying crates.

no-std-minimal-runtime.comment: Requires std; no no_std or deliberately minimal-runtime mode.

client-side-rendering-spa.comment: Seed's defining mode: a client-rendered WASM SPA served via 'trunk serve'.

server-side-rendering.comment: README 'Why not Seed?' explicitly lists 'No server-side rendering yet' (tracking issue #232).

static-site-generation.comment: No static-site-generation tooling; client-side rendering only.

hydration.comment: With no SSR, there is no server-rendered markup to hydrate.

server-functions.comment: No integrated isomorphic server-function mechanism; the server_integration example wires up client/server communication manually (fetch to a separate backend).

streaming-ssr.comment: No SSR at all, hence no streaming SSR.

backend-integration.comment: Frontend-only framework with no full-stack mode; it pairs with any backend over HTTP (the server_integration example demonstrates talking to a separate Rust server).

isomorphic-routing.comment: Routing runs client-side only; with no SSR there is no server-side route resolution.

official-router.comment: URL routing is built into Seed core (the Url type plus UrlChanged/UrlRequested subscriptions handled in update); no separate crate needed. A community derive-based router (seed_routing, seed-rs org) exists for typed routes.

router-name.comment: Routing lives in seed core (seed::browser::Url + url-change subscriptions) rather than a named router crate; the optional companion crate is seed_routing.

nested-routes.comment: The pages / pages_keep_state / page_trait examples build nested page hierarchies by matching successive Url path segments.

dynamic-route-params.comment: Route parameters are read by parsing Url path segments / query in the update handler.

typed-routes.comment: Core routing matches Url path segments imperatively (string-based); an invalid route is not a compile error. The community seed_routing crate adds derive/enum-based typed routes, but that is not part of core Seed.

route-guards.comment: Core Seed has no dedicated navigation-guard hook; redirect logic is written by hand in the update loop. The optional seed_routing crate offers guard support.

lazy-code-split-routes.comment: No first-party lazy / code-split route loading; the app compiles to a single WASM bundle.

styling-approach.comment: Inline styles via the style!{} macro and plain external CSS work out of the box; CSS-in-Rust with global and scoped styles is provided by the companion Seed Styles crate (styles_hooks), not seed core.

scoped-styles.comment: Component-scoped styling is available through the companion Seed Styles crate ('global and scoped styles'); it is not built into seed core.

component-library-ecosystem.comment: README 'Why not Seed?': 'Pre-built components are rare. You will likely have to roll your own components such as date pickers.' Essentially no ready-made UI component-library crates.

forms-inputs.comment: Inputs are controlled: value bound from the Model and updated via input_ev handlers dispatching a Msg. No two-way binding directive or dedicated form-helper library.

svg-canvas-support.comment: SVG elements can be authored with the element macros (svg![], path![], etc.) and there is a dedicated canvas example driving an HTML canvas via web-sys.

suspense-loading-ui.comment: No declarative suspense / loading-boundary primitive; loading states are modeled by hand in the Model.

error-boundaries.comment: No error-boundary component; error handling is done in the update loop.

documentation-quality.comment: Extensive example set and reasonable docs.rs API docs, but the main guide/tutorial site (seed-rs.org) is currently offline (domain issue #126), so the historically strong prose guide is not reachable; net adequate.

api-ergonomics.comment: Clean, idiomatic macro views that work with rustfmt/clippy and readable Elm-architecture flow; some boilerplate from Msg enums and manual update wiring keeps it below a 5.

compile-times.comment: Macro-based views but a comparatively lean framework; on par with peer macro-driven Rust web frameworks for a small app.

examples-count.comment: About 39 example projects in the repo (counter, pages, url, canvas, fetch, graphql, websocket, todomvc, etc.), well above the >20 'extensive' band.

interactive-playground.comment: No online playground/REPL or hosted example gallery; the project website that once hosted live examples (seed-rs.org) is currently disabled.

browser-devtools.comment: No dedicated Seed devtools/inspector beyond generic browser tools.

testing-support.comment: The tests example uses wasm-bindgen-test to run in-browser/headless tests; no dedicated component-testing harness.

github-stars.comment: ~3.8k stars per the GitHub API; checked 2026-07-19.

crates-io-downloads.comment: All-time downloads of the seed crate per crates.io API; checked 2026-07-19.

contributors.comment: ~61 contributors per the GitHub contributors API (including anonymous); checked 2026-07-19.

community-size.comment: A Discord server exists and there were ~61 historical contributors, but the project is now unmaintained with little current activity; effectively small/dormant today.

ecosystem-crates.comment: A few companion crates exist (Seed Styles / styles_hooks, seed_routing) and an awesome-seed-rs list, but the ecosystem is small and has stalled along with the framework.

notable-production-users.comment: No widely-documented production users; the framework is feature-complete but now unmaintained and no notable shipping apps are prominently cited.

meta-framework-full-stack-kit.comment: No full-stack meta-framework; Seed is frontend-only (no SSR/server functions), typically paired with a separate Rust backend.
